hook_text_english: 10 things happened in AI this week. Each one would have been the biggest story of the year a decade ago.
full_text_english: 10 things happened in AI this week. Each one would have been the biggest story of the year a decade ago.→ China blocked Meta's $17,000 crore acquisition of Manus AI.→ Sam Altman is building an AI-first smartphone with Jony Ive.→ OpenAI shipped GPT-5.5 with a new prompting guide that says stop writing prompts the old way.→ Manus launched Cloud Computer. Your AI agents now run 24/7 in the cloud.→ Telegram launched Lobster Father. Anyone can build an AI agent inside Telegram with zero code.→ OpenAI and Microsoft renegotiated their partnership. OpenAI can now ship across AWS and Google Cloud.→ Anthropic shipped Claude Connectors with Adobe and 50+ creative tools. Edit photos and resize videos directly in the chat.→ Google signed a $200M classified Pentagon AI deal. 600 employees signed an open letter against it.→ Replit launched Slides. ElevenLabs launched Music. Lovable launched Mobile.The map of AI is changing every single week now.I broke down every single one of these stories in detail in my latest video.Watch the full breakdown here ↓https://lnkd.in/g_sp96uF
body_summary_english: A rapid-fire list of major AI developments including China blocking Meta's acquisition, Sam Altman's AI phone with Jony Ive, GPT-5.5 launch, and partnerships between OpenAI, Microsoft, AWS, and Google Cloud. Each breakthrough demonstrates how dramatically the pace of AI innovation has accelerated.

transcript: Your dog could now live long enough to see your kids grow up. One injection just made that possible. Welcome to day 33 of future tech updates. A biotech company in San Francisco just got America's drug regulator to accept something that has never been accepted before. A drug that does not cure a disease. It slows down aging itself. The catch, it is only for dogs. Here is the science and it is genuinely fascinating. A great Dane lives around 7 to 10 years. A Chihuahua lives 14 to 16. Same species, almost double the lifespan. Nobody ever questioned why. The answer is one hormone called IGF-1. Big dogs have up to 28 times more of it than small dogs. It makes them grow huge, but it burns them out way faster. A company called Loyal built a drug that lowers this hormone in adult dogs. For big breeds, one injection at the vet every 3 to 6 months. For older dogs, a daily pill you give at home. They have tested it on over 1,300 dogs. The regulator has signed off. The first prescription could be written as early as 2027. Once it clears, this becomes as routine as vaccines for big breeds. Now, here is the part nobody is saying out loud. For the first time in history, a drug regulator has accepted that aging itself can be treated. Not a disease caused by aging. And the same hormone that burns out your Great Dane runs through every human body on Earth. Your dog might be the first patient, you could be the second. I share updates like these in my WhatsApp community. Link in bio.

transcript: James Cameron wrote Avatar in 1994, but he waited 15 years to release it. Now, this is not because he was short of money or too busy, but because the technology to make the film was just not ready yet. And in the case of Bahubali, Rajamali's team made 15,000 handdrawn storyboards and used 600 VFX artists across 18 countries. Now, they did it that way because there was no other option but to spend that time [music] and money. But what if we did? Look, I'm not a filmmaker. I run companies with AI. But last week, I gave myself one stupid challenge. Could a guy like me, sitting [music] at his laptop in his bedroom, make something that could actually pass for Bollywood in a single night? I picked the hardest possible test, a fight scene. Because every fight scene you've ever loved needs months of training, hundreds of crew members, and high risk. My scene was made in one night using a tool called I'm going to run you through my process. Watch till the end to find out the incredible result or whether I can actually get entry into Bollywood. Let's go. One thing, this video has a lot of prompts, like a lot. Image prompts, video prompts, full scene descriptions. You are not going to be able to keep up by pausing and screenshotting. So, I made a detailed PDF with everything, every prompt, every tool link, step by step. I dropped it in my free WhatsApp community. Link is in the description. You have seen the headlines. Every AI tool launching right now is claiming to replace something. Replace Bollywood, replace designers, replace Hollywood altogether. But here is the honest question I had. I am not a filmmaker. So when a tool says we are replacing Hollywood, that does not mean anything to me. It is too abstract. Forget replacing Hollywood. Can I sitting at my laptop tonight actually make one shot from a real movie? Just one cinematic scene. The kind of shot you would see in a big budget film. That is the challenge. And before I could even start, I had to figure out something I genuinely never thought about. How do real movies actually get made? So I did what I always do when I want to understand something fast. I went to Claude. I gave it the question. Let me read you exactly what I asked. I am a complete non-filmmaker. Walk me through how real movies actually get made. Specifically, what happens before a single frame is shot? The planning phase, mood boards, storyboards, all of that. explain it like I am a 10-year-old and Claude gave me the entire pipeline. Pre-production, production, post-production, the whole thing. But two things in the response made me stop and stare. I went deeper. Asked Claude to break down the mood board and storyboard process specifically. And honestly, what I learned was wild. The mood board. This is the first thing every director makes. reference images, color palettes, costume sketches pinned to a wall or built in a digital folder. Before a single frame is shot, the mood board exists. The mood board is the movie before the movie exists. Then comes the storyboard. One specific shot of the movie drawn in detail before filming. And this is where my mind broke a little. Bahubali SS Rajamuli's team made 15,000 storyboard sketches by hand for that movie. 15,000. the highest for any Indian film ever made. One full year of pre-production just for the planning phase before the cameras even turned on and the timeline. This is the part that genuinely broke my brain. You watch a 2-hour movie. The filmmaker behind it spent years making it. Avatar, the James Cameron one. Cameron wrote his first treatment for Avatar in 1994. The film released in December 2009, 15 years from the first idea on paper to a single ticket being sold. The two hours you watched, that is the tip of an iceberg you cannot see. And here I was sitting alone at my laptop with a single question. Could AI actually do any of this, even one part of it? To find out, I needed three things. Number one, the world's best image AI. Number two, the world's best vi

transcript: This is how you can make any building explorable by AI. Open Flipbook, upload any image. I uploaded a photo of Vidhana Soudha in Bangalore. It generated a full visual page around it, the architecture, the history, the streets [music] nearby. Every pixel made by AI in real time. Then I clicked on a pillar. It zoomed into the carving and explained the design. I clicked on a window. It showed me what was inside the building. Traditional search needs you to know what you are looking for. Flipbook flips that. You click and wander. The answer becomes the interface itself. [music] Built by an ex-OpenAI researcher. Launched 6 days ago. Comment book and I'll send you the link to try it. I drop a new setup like this [music] every single day in my free WhatsApp community. Link in bio.

hook_text_english: The motion design industry is about to have a very uncomfortable conversation.
full_text_english: The motion design industry is about to have a very uncomfortable conversation.I made a 10-second Uber promotional brand video this week. Fully animated. Film grain, easing curves, playback bar with scrubbing.I built it with one prompt inside Claude.The kind of cut that takes a 3-person motion team a full week to produce. Done in the time it takes to write a paragraph.Here's the starter prompt 👇🏻Create a 10-second promotional brand video for [BRAND], 1920×1080 (16:9), as an animated HTML/React artifact. Look: [BRAND palette]. Premium and emotional tone. Geometric sans-serif display font paired with a mono font for small uppercase kickers. Polish: subtle film-grain overlay; easeOutCubic entries, easeInCubic exits, easeOutBack for the logo. Include a playback bar with play/pause and scrubbing.The full master prompt I used for the Uber cut, plus variations for product launches and founder intros, is inside my free AI WhatsApp community.Join here 👇🏻https://lnkd.in/gG2tUqkQ
body_summary_english: The author created a professional 10-second Uber promotional video with animation, film grain, and easing curves using a single Claude prompt—work that traditionally takes a 3-person team a week. This demonstrates AI's disruptive impact on creative production workflows.
comments: The PROMPTS framework just reframed everything for me.I'm an AI content writer, and I realized I was stuck at P — playing with too many tools instead of building a real stack.The shift from "using AI" to "orchestrating AI" is the actual unlock. Most people never make that jump.Skill → System → Business. This is the sequence nobody talks about clearly enough.Dropping my vote here: Full IIM A recording, please. The unfiltered version always hits harder than a scripted one.
Hey Noman Somrose! I don't have the recording personally — I was actually voting for the original poster to share it! 😄 You can drop your request directly on their post, they might share it soon!"

AI isn't going to replace Leaders; Leaders who build AI systems are going to replace Leaders who don't. The PROMPTS framework isn't a tech manual—it is a blueprint for personal and professional sovereignty.

Awesome framework, Vaibhav! The *PROMPT* method—_Pick your toolkit_, _Refine_, _Open your world to AI_—is a game‑changer for anyone looking to actually leverage AI in everyday business. Loved the tip to focus on a tight stack of 5–6 tools instead of juggling dozens.👉 How have you seen the “Refine” step (tuning temperature & tokens) impact real‑world results for your clients or projects? 🚀

This really changed the way I look at AI.Most people are still treating AI like a tool to “try.”But the real shift happens when you start building systems around it instead of using it randomly.“Skill → System → Business” was probably the strongest part for me.

"Synthesize" is where most people quit too early. The shift from "6 different tools" to "a team of agents that work together" is what separates people who use AI from people who build with AI. What's the first orchestration setup you'd recommend for someone just starting out?

This is one of the most practical frameworks I’ve seen for approaching AI adoption Vaibhav Sisinty👏🚀A lot of people are still treating AI like a tool they occasionally use, while the real shift happens when AI becomes part of your workflow, systems, and decision-making process.Also loved the idea of becoming an “AI Generalist.”The future probably belongs to people who can combine domain expertise + AI leverage + problem-solving across functions instead of relying on just one technical skill.And this line stands out strongly:“Skill. System. Business.”That’s the real evolution AI is enabling — turning individual capability into scalable output 💯Also yes, a detailed YouTube breakdown of the PROMPTS framework would be super valuable 🔥

The 'S' for Synthesize is where the real ROI lies. Moving from 'using tools' to 'orchestrating teams of AI agents' is the shift I'm seeing in my own work. When you stop doing AI tasks and start letting AI run your pipelines, that’s when you truly scale. Great to see this being shared at IIM Ahmedabad.

Strong framework.Most people are currently stuck at the “tool discovery” stage — trying hundreds of AI apps without integrating them into actual workflows.The real value starts when AI moves from:assistance → automation → orchestration.That’s where productivity compounds.The most important point here is probably “Synthesize.”Using one AI tool is easy.Building connected systems where multiple AI agents handle research, content, operations, follow-ups, reporting, and execution together — that is where businesses will create disproportionate advantage.In the next few years, companies may not simply hire based on specialization alone.They will value people who can:• understand business problems• work across functions• use AI to increase output at scaleThat combination will define the next generation of high-leverage professionals and founders.Very relevant perspective for students and working professionals both.

Vaibhav Sisinty This really connected with me.When I started building solutions related to my own domain using AI, I realized we don’t always need deep coding knowledge to start building something meaningful.We just need clarity on the problem and the idea — AI helps us bring it to life 🚀

I'm stuck between P and R.How do you actually finalise your 5-6 without spending months testing each one? Is there a decision filter, like this tool wins for writing, this one for research, this one for automation or does the stack depend so heavily on the person's work type that there's no universal answer?And on the Refine step - temperature settings, max tokens, model differences - where does someone who isn't technical actually start? Is there a non-jargon way to understand what these levers actually do in practice, not just in definition?Would vote for the full IIM A recording as-is. Frameworks land better when you hear the context and the questions behind them.
The biggest shift is when people stop treating AI like a tool and start treating it like infrastructure. That’s where output really compounds.
As an IIM Ranchi MBA going deep into AI, this resonates hard. The B-school crowd has the strategy chops; what most lack is shipping discipline. Sessions like yours close that gap fast.

Spot on, Vaibhav! From a systemic perspective, the PROMPTS framework is the ultimate blueprint for protecting our Cognitive Bandwidth. When we shift from manually executing tasks to orchestrating AI agents, we drastically reduce our daily Systemic Friction. This transition from "tool user" to "system builder" effectively lowers our Allostatic Load, allowing the "Human Hardware" to focus purely on high-level synthesis rather than exhausting background processing. The future belongs to those who successfully automate their friction!

One thing I’m realising while building BWS is that the world is changing faster than traditional learning models can keep up with.Curiosity, adaptability, and the ability to learn continuously are becoming far more valuable than just having information. Vaibhav Sisinty
The biggest shift is not people using AI.It’s people learning how to turn their thinking into systems through AI.That’s where the real leverage starts compounding. Vaibhav Sisinty

We’re entering a phase where the gap between two people won’t be intelligence.It’ll be orchestration.One person using AI occasionally vs another building an entire cognitive operating system around it.Btw I agree with many others like me here. The raw recording hits completely different!

Powerful perspective, Vaibhav Sisinty! The distinction between 'knowing AI' and 'converting skill into a system' is what will separate the 10M solo-preneurs from the rest. Most people are stuck at the 'Pick your toolkit' phase; seeing a path toward 'Pipeline' and 'Synthesize' is eye-opening. I’d vote for the YouTube deep-dive to see the 'how' behind the framework!

Co-founded TriggerlyAI after several years inside large retail ops, building voice agents and automations for service businesses. The "Skill. System. Business." line is the same arc I see playing out with our clients now. The owner who wins is never the best operator in the business, it is the one who builds the system that runs the business when they are not in it. AI just collapsed the time it takes to build that system from years to weeks.
The people who benefit most from AI will probably be the ones who learn how to integrate it into daily workflows instead of treating it like a separate tool.

I am sure the energy in that room was electric. As someone deep in the AI and software space, I would love to know what was the one question from the IIM-A students regarding the future of tech that surprised you the most? Keep inspiring!
The shift from using AI to orchestrating AI is where the real leverage gap between people will open up.
This was genuinely insightful. The shift from “using AI” to “building systems with AI” is what most people still don’t understand. The PROMPTS framework makes it feel practical, not overwhelming.

The biggest trap in AI right now is getting stuck at "P" hoarding tools, while the real leverage is at "S" building systems. I'm currently wrestling with "Pipelines" to fully automate the manual stuff.Vote: Drop the raw IIM-A recording! The unfiltered audience Q&A always beats a scripted YouTube video.
The new productivity flex isn’t working harder. It’s having AI coworkers that don’t sleep.
The real advantage won’t come from just using AI tools it’ll come from turning your skills into systems that compound without you Vaibhav Sisinty
The future belongs to people who don’t just use AI tools, but build AI-powered systems that amplify their thinking, output, and execution across everything they do.
Most people are still collecting AI tools like trophies instead of building systems that genuinely save time, increase output, or solve real problems.
Most people are still treating AI like a smarter Google search. The “systems” part is where things start getting unfair.Vaibhav Sisinty
AI Generalist isn’t just a role, it’s a survival skill in the current tech shift.The faster you adapt, the more relevant you stay. Vaibhav Sisinty

commentsTranslated.0: The PROMPTS framework just reframed everything for me.I'm an AI content writer, and I realized I was stuck at P — playing with too many tools instead of building a real stack.The shift from "using AI" to "orchestrating AI" is the actual unlock. Most people never make that jump.Skill → System → Business. This is the sequence nobody talks about clearly enough.Dropping my vote here: Full IIM A recording, please. The unfiltered version always hits harder than a scripted one.
commentsTranslated.1: Hey Noman Somrose! I don't have the recording personally — I was actually voting for the original poster to share it! 😄 You can drop your request directly on their post, they might share it soon!"
commentsTranslated.2: AI isn't going to replace Leaders; Leaders who build AI systems are going to replace Leaders who don't. The PROMPTS framework isn't a tech manual—it is a blueprint for personal and professional sovereignty.
commentsTranslated.3: Awesome framework, Vaibhav! The *PROMPT* method—_Pick your toolkit_, _Refine_, _Open your world to AI_—is a game‑changer for anyone looking to actually leverage AI in everyday business. Loved the tip to focus on a tight stack of 5–6 tools instead of juggling dozens.👉 How have you seen the "Refine" step (tuning temperature & tokens) impact real‑world results for your clients or projects? 🚀
commentsTranslated.4: This really changed the way I look at AI.Most people are still treating AI like a tool to "try."But the real shift happens when you start building systems around it instead of using it randomly."Skill → System → Business" was probably the strongest part for me.
commentsTranslated.6: This is one of the most practical frameworks I've seen for approaching AI adoption Vaibhav Sisinty👏🚀A lot of people are still treating AI like a tool they occasionally use, while the real shift happens when AI becomes part of your workflow, systems, and decision-making process.Also loved the idea of becoming an "AI Generalist."The future probably belongs to people who can combine domain expertise + AI leverage + problem-solving across functions instead of relying on just one technical skill.And this line stands out strongly:"Skill. System. Business."That's the real evolution AI is enabling — turning individual capability into scalable output 💯Also yes, a detailed YouTube breakdown of the PROMPTS framework would be super valuable 🔥
commentsTranslated.7: The 'S' for Synthesize is where the real ROI lies. Moving from 'using tools' to 'orchestrating teams of AI agents' is the shift I'm seeing in my own work. When you stop doing AI tasks and start letting AI run your pipelines, that's when you truly scale. Great to see this being shared at IIM Ahmedabad.
commentsTranslated.8: Strong framework.Most people are currently stuck at the "tool discovery" stage — trying hundreds of AI apps without integrating them into actual workflows.The real value starts when AI moves from:assistance → automation → orchestration.That's where productivity compounds.The most important point here is probably "Synthesize."Using one AI tool is easy.Building connected systems where multiple AI agents handle research, content, operations, follow-ups, reporting, and execution together — that is where businesses will create disproportionate advantage.In the next few years, companies may not simply hire based on specialization alone.They will value people who can:• understand business problems• work across functions• use AI to increase output at scaleThat combination will define the next generation of high-leverage professionals and founders.Very relevant perspective for students and working professionals both.

hook_text_english: I built an AI agent that wakes up at 9 AM, reads Hacker News, and drops a PDF on my Telegram before I have my morning coffee.
full_text_english: I built an AI agent that wakes up at 9 AM, reads Hacker News, and drops a PDF on my Telegram before I have my morning coffee.It's called Hermes. Open source. Runs on its own server.A year ago, the work I'm now giving Hermes needed three people on my team. Almost 2 lakh a month.I gave Hermes four jobs:→ Daily Hacker News digest as a PDF→ Competitor intel on OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and Grok every morning→ YouTube thumbnail grids in 90 seconds→ Transcripts pulled from my channel and turned into scripts in my voiceEvery task it finishes, it writes a note for itself. Next time, it runs in 5 seconds flat.ChatGPT doesn't do this. Claude doesn't do this.Full step by step demo on YouTube ↓ https://lnkd.in/g2HF9zki
body_summary_english: The author created Hermes, an open-source AI agent that automates four daily tasks (Hacker News digest, competitor intel, YouTube thumbnails, transcripts-to-scripts) that previously required three full-time employees at 200k/month. The agent self-optimizes with each run.

"Synthesize" is where most people quit too early. The shift from "6 different tools" to "a team of agents that work together" is what separates people who use AI from people who build with AI. What's the first orchestration setup you'd recommend for someone just starting out?

Vaibhav Sisinty This really connected with me.When I started building solutions related to my own domain using AI, I realized we don’t always need deep coding knowledge to start building something meaningful.We just need clarity on the problem and the idea — AI helps us bring it to life 🚀

I'm stuck between P and R.How do you actually finalise your 5-6 without spending months testing each one? Is there a decision filter, like this tool wins for writing, this one for research, this one for automation or does the stack depend so heavily on the person's work type that there's no universal answer?And on the Refine step - temperature settings, max tokens, model differences - where does someone who isn't technical actually start? Is there a non-jargon way to understand what these levers actually do in practice, not just in definition?Would vote for the full IIM A recording as-is. Frameworks land better when you hear the context and the questions behind them.
The biggest shift is when people stop treating AI like a tool and start treating it like infrastructure. That’s where output really compounds.
As an IIM Ranchi MBA going deep into AI, this resonates hard. The B-school crowd has the strategy chops; what most lack is shipping discipline. Sessions like yours close that gap fast.

Spot on, Vaibhav! From a systemic perspective, the PROMPTS framework is the ultimate blueprint for protecting our Cognitive Bandwidth. When we shift from manually executing tasks to orchestrating AI agents, we drastically reduce our daily Systemic Friction. This transition from "tool user" to "system builder" effectively lowers our Allostatic Load, allowing the "Human Hardware" to focus purely on high-level synthesis rather than exhausting background processing. The future belongs to those who successfully automate their friction!

Co-founded TriggerlyAI after several years inside large retail ops, building voice agents and automations for service businesses. The "Skill. System. Business." line is the same arc I see playing out with our clients now. The owner who wins is never the best operator in the business, it is the one who builds the system that runs the business when they are not in it. AI just collapsed the time it takes to build that system from years to weeks.
The people who benefit most from AI will probably be the ones who learn how to integrate it into daily workflows instead of treating it like a separate tool.

I am sure the energy in that room was electric. As someone deep in the AI and software space, I would love to know what was the one question from the IIM-A students regarding the future of tech that surprised you the most? Keep inspiring!
The shift from using AI to orchestrating AI is where the real leverage gap between people will open up.

Mark Zuckerberg's latest move is truly ambitious, with Meta signing a deal for space-based solar power. This initiative with Overview Energy aims to provide 1 gigawatt of continuous solar power beamed from space, showcasing a major step in renewable energy. This development is certainly big tech news, pushing the boundaries of what's possible in solar energy.

[music] A company called Overview Energy is putting solar panels in orbit, 22,000 miles above Earth. In space, the sun never sets, so power flows non-stop. Satellites [music] beam that energy straight down to the ground, and Meta has already booked 1 gigawatt of it, enough to power a small Indian city from space. But that is only half the deal. Meta also signed with a company called Noon Energy that builds [music] batteries unlike anything you have seen. Tesla batteries last about 4 hours. Noon batteries last 100 hours straight. So, even if the grid goes down for 4 full days, >> [music] >> Meta's AI never stops running. Space solar for unlimited power, 100-hour batteries for unlimited [music] backup. Both signed to one company to keep AI running non-stop. Now, here is the bigger picture that nobody is connecting. 2 years ago, AI ran on normal electricity. Last year, companies started buying nuclear plants. This year, they ran out of options on Earth, so they are leaving the planet, and Meta is not alone. Elon Musk [music] has filed plans to put data centers in orbit, not on Earth, in space. The AI race is no longer about who builds the smartest model. [music] It is about who controls the energy, and Earth alone is no longer enough. That is the real race now, and it just left the ground. I share [music] updates like these in my WhatsApp community. hook_text_english: 94 out of every 100 people will quit AI at the exact same stage.
full_text_english: 94 out of every 100 people will quit AI at the exact same stage.They'll touch 40 tools. They'll never pick 3.Three years from now they'll be saying "AI is overhyped" while their coworker is making far more at the next desk.I've trained people across 150+ countries on AI. The same 5 stages show up every single time.I built a 5-letter framework that takes anyone from AI Resistor to AI Orchestrator.One person at the final stage out-produces a team stuck at stage 2.There are job roles being created right now that didn't exist 18 months ago. Most of them pay more than traditional roles in the same company.The window for early movers is open. It closes the moment everyone else catches up.I broke down all 5 stages with the exact tools, frameworks, and real-world examples in my latest video.Watch the full breakdown here ↓https://lnkd.in/gM4wPMnK
body_summary_english: The author claims to have trained 150+ countries and identified 5 stages of AI adoption, with most people failing to specialize and instead trying 40+ tools. A framework is implied that moves people from 'AI Resistor' to 'AI Orchestrator,' with top performers vastly outproducing those at earlier stages.
comments: The PROMPTS framework just reframed everything for me.I'm an AI content writer, and I realized I was stuck at P — playing with too many tools instead of building a real stack.The shift from "using AI" to "orchestrating AI" is the actual unlock. Most people never make that jump.Skill → System → Business. This is the sequence nobody talks about clearly enough.Dropping my vote here: Full IIM A recording, please. The unfiltered version always hits harder than a scripted one.
Hey Noman Somrose! I don't have the recording personally — I was actually voting for the original poster to share it! 😄 You can drop your request directly on their post, they might share it soon!"

AI isn't going to replace Leaders; Leaders who build AI systems are going to replace Leaders who don't. The PROMPTS framework isn't a tech manual—it is a blueprint for personal and professional sovereignty.

Awesome framework, Vaibhav! The *PROMPT* method—_Pick your toolkit_, _Refine_, _Open your world to AI_—is a game‑changer for anyone looking to actually leverage AI in everyday business. Loved the tip to focus on a tight stack of 5–6 tools instead of juggling dozens.👉 How have you seen the “Refine” step (tuning temperature & tokens) impact real‑world results for your clients or projects? 🚀

This really changed the way I look at AI.Most people are still treating AI like a tool to “try.”But the real shift happens when you start building systems around it instead of using it randomly.“Skill → System → Business” was probably the strongest part for me.

"Synthesize" is where most people quit too early. The shift from "6 different tools" to "a team of agents that work together" is what separates people who use AI from people who build with AI. What's the first orchestration setup you'd recommend for someone just starting out?

This is one of the most practical frameworks I’ve seen for approaching AI adoption Vaibhav Sisinty👏🚀A lot of people are still treating AI like a tool they occasionally use, while the real shift happens when AI becomes part of your workflow, systems, and decision-making process.Also loved the idea of becoming an “AI Generalist.”The future probably belongs to people who can combine domain expertise + AI leverage + problem-solving across functions instead of relying on just one technical skill.And this line stands out strongly:“Skill. System. Business.”That’s the real evolution AI is enabling — turning individual capability into scalable output 💯Also yes, a detailed YouTube breakdown of the PROMPTS framework would be super valuable 🔥

The 'S' for Synthesize is where the real ROI lies. Moving from 'using tools' to 'orchestrating teams of AI agents' is the shift I'm seeing in my own work. When you stop doing AI tasks and start letting AI run your pipelines, that’s when you truly scale. Great to see this being shared at IIM Ahmedabad.

Strong framework.Most people are currently stuck at the “tool discovery” stage — trying hundreds of AI apps without integrating them into actual workflows.The real value starts when AI moves from:assistance → automation → orchestration.That’s where productivity compounds.The most important point here is probably “Synthesize.”Using one AI tool is easy.Building connected systems where multiple AI agents handle research, content, operations, follow-ups, reporting, and execution together — that is where businesses will create disproportionate advantage.In the next few years, companies may not simply hire based on specialization alone.They will value people who can:• understand business problems• work across functions• use AI to increase output at scaleThat combination will define the next generation of high-leverage professionals and founders.Very relevant perspective for students and working professionals both.

Vaibhav Sisinty This really connected with me.When I started building solutions related to my own domain using AI, I realized we don’t always need deep coding knowledge to start building something meaningful.We just need clarity on the problem and the idea — AI helps us bring it to life 🚀

I'm stuck between P and R.How do you actually finalise your 5-6 without spending months testing each one? Is there a decision filter, like this tool wins for writing, this one for research, this one for automation or does the stack depend so heavily on the person's work type that there's no universal answer?And on the Refine step - temperature settings, max tokens, model differences - where does someone who isn't technical actually start? Is there a non-jargon way to understand what these levers actually do in practice, not just in definition?Would vote for the full IIM A recording as-is. Frameworks land better when you hear the context and the questions behind them.
The biggest shift is when people stop treating AI like a tool and start treating it like infrastructure. That’s where output really compounds.
As an IIM Ranchi MBA going deep into AI, this resonates hard. The B-school crowd has the strategy chops; what most lack is shipping discipline. Sessions like yours close that gap fast.

Spot on, Vaibhav! From a systemic perspective, the PROMPTS framework is the ultimate blueprint for protecting our Cognitive Bandwidth. When we shift from manually executing tasks to orchestrating AI agents, we drastically reduce our daily Systemic Friction. This transition from "tool user" to "system builder" effectively lowers our Allostatic Load, allowing the "Human Hardware" to focus purely on high-level synthesis rather than exhausting background processing. The future belongs to those who successfully automate their friction!

One thing I’m realising while building BWS is that the world is changing faster than traditional learning models can keep up with.Curiosity, adaptability, and the ability to learn continuously are becoming far more valuable than just having information. Vaibhav Sisinty
The biggest shift is not people using AI.It’s people learning how to turn their thinking into systems through AI.That’s where the real leverage starts compounding. Vaibhav Sisinty

hook_text_english: The man who designed the iPhone just got paid $6.5B to kill it.
full_text_english: The man who designed the iPhone just got paid $6.5B to kill it.Sam Altman is building a secret device. The plan leaked yesterday. An AI-first phone with no apps. Agents do everything for you. Every tech giant who tried this has failed.Zuckerberg's Facebook Phone died in weeks. Bezos burned hundreds of millions on the Fire Phone. Because a phone isn't a product. It's an ecosystem. No apps means no buyers. No buyers means no developers. That kills every challenger.Apple took 20 years to build that ecosystem. Altman wants it by 2028.Here's the part most people miss.OpenAI already lives inside every iPhone and Android. They don't need a device to win the AI race.But if you want to build the largest company in the world, you eventually have to do what the largest company does.Apple is worth $3 trillion because of the ecosystem they own.AI alone might not get you there. You need the device.--I share updates like these in my free AI community on WhatsApp.Join here ↓ https://lnkd.in/gG2tUqkQ
body_summary_english: Sam Altman is building a secretive AI-first phone with Jony Ive (leaked details), designed without apps where AI agents handle all tasks. The post analyzes why previous tech giant phone attempts failed and how OpenAI's ecosystem positioning might succeed where Facebook Phone and Fire Phone did not.

commentsTranslated.8: Strong framework.Most people are currently stuck at the "tool discovery" stage — trying hundreds of AI apps without integrating them into actual workflows.The real value starts when AI moves from:assistance → automation → orchestration.That's where productivity compounds.The most important point here is probably "Synthesize."Using one AI tool is easy.Building connected systems where multiple AI agents handle research, content, operations, follow-ups, reporting, and execution together — that is where businesses will create disproportionate advantage.In the next few years, companies may not simply hire based on specialization alone.They will value people who can:• understand business problems• work across functions• use AI to increase output at scaleThat combination will define the next generation of high-leverage professionals and founders.Very relevant perspective for students and working professionals both.
commentsTranslated.9: Vaibhav Sisinty This really connected with me.When I started building solutions related to my own domain using AI, I realized we don't always need deep coding knowledge to start building something meaningful.We just need clarity on the problem and the idea — AI helps us bring it to life 🚀
commentsTranslated.11: The biggest shift is when people stop treating AI like a tool and start treating it like infrastructure. That's where output really compounds.
commentsTranslated.14: One thing I'm realising while building BWS is that the world is changing faster than traditional learning models can keep up with.Curiosity, adaptability, and the ability to learn continuously are becoming far more valuable than just having information. Vaibhav Sisinty
commentsTranslated.15: The biggest shift is not people using AI.It's people learning how to turn their thinking into systems through AI.That's where the real leverage starts compounding. Vaibhav Sisinty
commentsTranslated.16: We're entering a phase where the gap between two people won't be intelligence.It'll be orchestration.One person using AI occasionally vs another building an entire cognitive operating system around it.Btw I agree with many others like me here. The raw recording hits completely different!
commentsTranslated.17: Powerful perspective, Vaibhav Sisinty! The distinction between 'knowing AI' and 'converting skill into a system' is what will separate the 10M solo-preneurs from the rest. commentsTranslated.24: The new productivity flex isn't working harder. It's having AI coworkers that don't sleep.

hook_text_english: OpenAI just launched the tool that's going to replace ChatGPT.
full_text_english: OpenAI just launched the tool that's going to replace ChatGPT. 🤯And it's made by OpenAI.Last week, they quietly dropped a new app called Codex. Most people heard the name and assumed it was a developer thing.It's not. It's an app you install like Spotify. No terminal. No setup.And one moment with it broke my brain.I was building an app. Codex finished the build. Then on its own, without me telling it to, it started testing it on desktop and mobile.It noticed a bug on the mobile version.So it stopped. Made a plan to fix it. Ran the fix. Re-tested. Verified it worked.Twenty seconds. I was in the kitchen.That's not a tool. That's a teammate.ChatGPT mostly talks to you. Codex talks to you AND does things for you. It opens your apps.It runs software. It builds. It tests. It catches its own mistakes.Five years ago, what I built in 4 minutes would have cost me $3,000 and 2 weeks with a designer.If you've had a side project sitting in your notes app for two years, the reason you didn't start it just stopped existing.The skill is not the tool anymore. The skill is knowing what to ask.I broke down the entire workflow in my latest video. Watch the full breakdown here ↓https://lnkd.in/gWJ3BpyC
body_summary_english: OpenAI quietly released Codex, a new consumer app (not developer-focused) that autonomously completed a software build, tested it, identified bugs, fixed them, and re-tested—all without explicit instruction. The agent demonstrated independent problem-solving capabilities beyond traditional tool usage.
comments: The PROMPTS framework just reframed everything for me.I'm an AI content writer, and I realized I was stuck at P — playing with too many tools instead of building a real stack.The shift from "using AI" to "orchestrating AI" is the actual unlock. Most people never make that jump.Skill → System → Business. This is the sequence nobody talks about clearly enough.Dropping my vote here: Full IIM A recording, please. The unfiltered version always hits harder than a scripted one.
Hey Noman Somrose! I don't have the recording personally — I was actually voting for the original poster to share it! 😄 You can drop your request directly on their post, they might share it soon!"

AI isn't going to replace Leaders; Leaders who build AI systems are going to replace Leaders who don't. The PROMPTS framework isn't a tech manual—it is a blueprint for personal and professional sovereignty.

Awesome framework, Vaibhav! The *PROMPT* method—_Pick your toolkit_, _Refine_, _Open your world to AI_—is a game‑changer for anyone looking to actually leverage AI in everyday business. Loved the tip to focus on a tight stack of 5–6 tools instead of juggling dozens.👉 How have you seen the “Refine” step (tuning temperature & tokens) impact real‑world results for your clients or projects? 🚀

This really changed the way I look at AI.Most people are still treating AI like a tool to “try.”But the real shift happens when you start building systems around it instead of using it randomly.“Skill → System → Business” was probably the strongest part for me.

"Synthesize" is where most people quit too early. The shift from "6 different tools" to "a team of agents that work together" is what separates people who use AI from people who build with AI. What's the first orchestration setup you'd recommend for someone just starting out?

This is one of the most practical frameworks I’ve seen for approaching AI adoption Vaibhav Sisinty👏🚀A lot of people are still treating AI like a tool they occasionally use, while the real shift happens when AI becomes part of your workflow, systems, and decision-making process.Also loved the idea of becoming an “AI Generalist.”The future probably belongs to people who can combine domain expertise + AI leverage + problem-solving across functions instead of relying on just one technical skill.And this line stands out strongly:“Skill. System. Business.”That’s the real evolution AI is enabling — turning individual capability into scalable output 💯Also yes, a detailed YouTube breakdown of the PROMPTS framework would be super valuable 🔥

The 'S' for Synthesize is where the real ROI lies. Moving from 'using tools' to 'orchestrating teams of AI agents' is the shift I'm seeing in my own work. When you stop doing AI tasks and start letting AI run your pipelines, that’s when you truly scale. Great to see this being shared at IIM Ahmedabad.

Strong framework.Most people are currently stuck at the “tool discovery” stage — trying hundreds of AI apps without integrating them into actual workflows.The real value starts when AI moves from:assistance → automation → orchestration.That’s where productivity compounds.The most important point here is probably “Synthesize.”Using one AI tool is easy.Building connected systems where multiple AI agents handle research, content, operations, follow-ups, reporting, and execution together — that is where businesses will create disproportionate advantage.In the next few years, companies may not simply hire based on specialization alone.They will value people who can:• understand business problems• work across functions• use AI to increase output at scaleThat combination will define the next generation of high-leverage professionals and founders.Very relevant perspective for students and working professionals both.

Vaibhav Sisinty This really connected with me.When I started building solutions related to my own domain using AI, I realized we don’t always need deep coding knowledge to start building something meaningful.We just need clarity on the problem and the idea — AI helps us bring it to life 🚀

I'm stuck between P and R.How do you actually finalise your 5-6 without spending months testing each one? Is there a decision filter, like this tool wins for writing, this one for research, this one for automation or does the stack depend so heavily on the person's work type that there's no universal answer?And on the Refine step - temperature settings, max tokens, model differences - where does someone who isn't technical actually start? Is there a non-jargon way to understand what these levers actually do in practice, not just in definition?Would vote for the full IIM A recording as-is. Frameworks land better when you hear the context and the questions behind them.
The biggest shift is when people stop treating AI like a tool and start treating it like infrastructure. That’s where output really compounds.
As an IIM Ranchi MBA going deep into AI, this resonates hard. The B-school crowd has the strategy chops; what most lack is shipping discipline. Sessions like yours close that gap fast.

Spot on, Vaibhav! From a systemic perspective, the PROMPTS framework is the ultimate blueprint for protecting our Cognitive Bandwidth. When we shift from manually executing tasks to orchestrating AI agents, we drastically reduce our daily Systemic Friction. This transition from "tool user" to "system builder" effectively lowers our Allostatic Load, allowing the "Human Hardware" to focus purely on high-level synthesis rather than exhausting background processing. The future belongs to those who successfully automate their friction!

One thing I’m realising while building BWS is that the world is changing faster than traditional learning models can keep up with.Curiosity, adaptability, and the ability to learn continuously are becoming far more valuable than just having information. Vaibhav Sisinty
The biggest shift is not people using AI.It’s people learning how to turn their thinking into systems through AI.That’s where the real leverage starts compounding. Vaibhav Sisinty

We’re entering a phase where the gap between two people won’t be intelligence.It’ll be orchestration.One person using AI occasionally vs another building an entire cognitive operating system around it.Btw I agree with many others like me here. The raw recording hits completely different!

Powerful perspective, Vaibhav Sisinty! The distinction between 'knowing AI' and 'converting skill into a system' is what will separate the 10M solo-preneurs from the rest. Most people are stuck at the 'Pick your toolkit' phase; seeing a path toward 'Pipeline' and 'Synthesize' is eye-opening. I’d vote for the YouTube deep-dive to see the 'how' behind the framework!

Co-founded TriggerlyAI after several years inside large retail ops, building voice agents and automations for service businesses. The "Skill. System. Business." line is the same arc I see playing out with our clients now. The owner who wins is never the best operator in the business, it is the one who builds the system that runs the business when they are not in it. AI just collapsed the time it takes to build that system from years to weeks.
The people who benefit most from AI will probably be the ones who learn how to integrate it into daily workflows instead of treating it like a separate tool.

I am sure the energy in that room was electric. As someone deep in the AI and software space, I would love to know what was the one question from the IIM-A students regarding the future of tech that surprised you the most? Keep inspiring!
The shift from using AI to orchestrating AI is where the real leverage gap between people will open up.
This was genuinely insightful. The shift from “using AI” to “building systems with AI” is what most people still don’t understand. The PROMPTS framework makes it feel practical, not overwhelming.

The biggest trap in AI right now is getting stuck at "P" hoarding tools, while the real leverage is at "S" building systems. I'm currently wrestling with "Pipelines" to fully automate the manual stuff.Vote: Drop the raw IIM-A recording! The unfiltered audience Q&A always beats a scripted YouTube video.

hook_text_english: Your Instagram is now self-analyzing. Connect your IG to Manus and it unlocks your data.
full_text_english: Your Instagram is now self-analyzingConnect your IG to Manus and it unlocks your data. Posts, reach, engagement. Everything.Then I ran one prompt and it broke down what’s actually working. Hooks getting saves, reels converting followers, topics to double down on and what to drop.A full content strategy built from your own data.I have dropped prompts and more resources like this in my free community Join here👇 https://lnkd.in/gG2tUqkQ
body_summary_english: Manus allows Instagram account connection to analyze posts, reach, engagement, and extract actionable content strategy insights via AI prompt analysis. The author promotes a free community as a resource hub via link in post.

hook_text_english: I gave Claude one Excel file. It built me an entire finance dashboard.
full_text_english: I gave Claude one Excel file. It built me an entire finance dashboard.Not kidding.Took a sample P&L.One prompt at a time.55 minutes total.Here is what came out:1. Budget vs Actuals Dashboard (15 mins)→ Read every transaction note.→ Pulled out company names automatically.→ Costs in red, revenue in green.→ Every formula, every format done.2. Full Year Reforecast (28 mins)→ Took Q1 numbers + the original budget.→ Built a side-by-side comparison.→ Added a "why this changed" column for every line.→ I never asked for that column. It just knew.3. Valuation Model + Sensitivity Table (12 mins)→ 7x7 grid of all possible scenarios.→ Base case highlighted automatically.→ Every cell linked with real formulas.→ Change one number — everything updates.Here is the thing.This isn't a fancy enterprise tool.It's just Claude Pro + Excel.Btw, the magic isn't the AI.It's the prompts.Each one took 30+ tries to nail.Want the full step-by-step process I used to build all three?Join my community using the link below.https://lnkd.in/gG2tUqkQ
body_summary_english: The author fed Claude a P&L statement and through iterative prompts (55 minutes total), Claude built three complex financial outputs: Budget vs Actuals Dashboard, Full Year Reforecast, and Valuation Model with Sensitivity Analysis—demonstrating autonomous financial modeling capability.

full_text_english: Microsoft just quietly built something nobody's talking about.A piece of glass that stores your data for 10,000 years. 🤯They just figured out how to store 4.84 terabytes on a piece of glass the size of a coaster. Same glass as your kitchen Pyrex.A femtosecond laser burns microscopic dots into 300 layers inside 2 millimeters of glass. The 1978 Superman movie is already saved on one slab.You can boil it. Flood it. Hit it with magnets. Bake it at 500 degrees. The data sits there for 10,000 years using zero electricity, zero cooling, zero maintenance.Every cloud server today is a building that has to stay alive to keep your data alive. Microsoft just built the opposite. A coaster that outlives the building.--I share updates like these in my free AI community on WhatsApp.Join here ↓https://lnkd.in/gG2tUqkQ
body_summary_english: Microsoft developed a femtosecond laser technology that stores 4.84 terabytes on Pyrex-like glass (coaster-sized), demonstrating extreme durability (boiling, flooding, magnets, 500°F) with zero electricity/cooling/maintenance, already storing the 1978 Superman movie. This contrasts with energy-intensive cloud infrastructure.

hook_text_english: OpenAI just dropped a free prompting guide for GPT 5.5
full_text_english:
🔗 Join our WhatsApp Community
Get the latest AI updates, tips, and insights straight to your inbox:
https://go.stayingahead.com/YT22

OpenAI just dropped a free prompting guide for GPT 5.5 with 4 specific rules
that contradict everything every prompting course has taught for the last
two years. We're breaking it down before they pull it.

Plus 18 more AI updates from this week. China blocked Zuckerberg's biggest AI
buyout ever. Sam Altman teamed up with Steve Jobs's top designer to build the
iPhone killer. Anthropic just turned Claude into a creative tool that runs
Adobe inside the chat. And xAI launched Grok Imagine Agent Beta, where the
official launch video itself was made by AI.

Full Grok Imagine Agent Beta tutorial at the end of this video.

01:17 China blocked Zuckerberg's biggest AI buyout
02:08 Sam Altman x Steve Jobs's iPhone designer
02:54 Claude now runs Adobe inside the chat
03:24 Google's $200M Pentagon deal (600 employees revolted)
04:08 Grok Imagine Agent Beta launch
04:52 Manus Cloud Computer
05:45 OpenAI x OpenClaw (use ChatGPT subscription as agent)
06:43 GPT 5.5 prompting guide they might delete
07:31 Replit Slides
08:11 Gemini creates Docs, Sheets, PDFs
08:40 ElevenLabs Music
09:12 Lovable goes mobile
09:44 Snapchat AI Sponsored Snaps
10:09 Telegram AI bots in 30 seconds
10:39 NotebookLM auto-label
11:15 OpenAI x Microsoft deal rewritten
11:56 ElevenFlows real-time collaboration
12:49 Google Translate pronunciation
13:11 Google Photos AI Wardrobe
14:03 GROK IMAGINE AGENT BETA - FULL TUTORIAL

🏢 Want to Train Your Team on AI?
My team and I have trained employees at companies like Adobe, Razorpay, Uber, and many more helping teams actually use AI to work faster and smarter. If you're a founder or leader who wants to upskill your team, reach out to us directly: 
https://stayingahead.ai/b2b

Mark Zuckerberg tried to buy a Chinese AI tool that's quietly beating GPT and Claude. [music] China just blocked the deal and Sam Altman just gave every ChatGPT user a free AI agent. [music] The same one Anthropic, ChatGPT's biggest rival, tried to shut down. This week Google just signed an AI deal with the US military so big 600 of their [music] own employees revolted. And one tool just turned your phone into an app builder. OpenAI just dropped a guide that changes >> [music] >> everything you know about prompting new ChatGPT. Plus 13 more updates dropped this week [music] and at the end of this video I'm breaking down what Elon Musk's XAI just shipped. The tool so powerful the official launch video itself [music] was made by AI. So let's get into it. Hi, I run three AI companies and the world's biggest free AI community of 1 [music] million people. It's called Staying Ahead. These AI updates go live here once a week, but our members get them on WhatsApp the moment they break, way before they hit YouTube. The free link is in the description. Now let's dive into the video. For the past four years Mark Zuckerberg and Meta have been lagging behind in the AI race. So he did what any billionaire does when he's losing. He tried to write a check. He tried to buy the AI that was quietly beating GPT and Claude for a whopping 17,000 crores. But there was one problem. The tool called Manas AI was built by a Chinese company and China being China, they weren't about to hand that technology over to Silicon Valley. So they blocked the deal and didn't let Zuckerberg buy it. We don't know where this ends up, but what we do know is that this tool is available right now and we wanted to explore it to see what all the hype is about. So you may be wondering what exactly is Manas AI. Think of it as Deep Research, Lovable, and Claude co-work, all rolled into one. It's not just a chatbot, it's an incredible agent, and it's widely considered better than anything OpenAI or Anthropic has put out so far. So, we made a video and tutorial about it, and it genuinely blew our minds. You can check out the video here. Sam Altman is reportedly planning to build a smartphone, an AI-first phone, and they want to compete with Apple directly. The device would reportedly run a custom OS where traditional apps are largely replaced by AI agents capable of handling tasks directly. What's more, the man who designed the iPhone, iPod, iMac, and every iconic Apple product for 30 years is joining Sam Altman on this project. Now, historically, entering the highly competitive smartphone market involves massive challenges. Zuckerberg tried the Facebook phone, Bezos tried the Amazon Fire phone, and they both failed. Let's hope Altman succeeds. Now, on to the next news. You can now use Adobe and 50 other Creative Cloud tools directly on Claude. Need to touch up some photos? Just drop them in, describe the outcome you want, and watch Adobe do the magic within Claude itself. You can ask it to make Instagram stories by using the Adobe Design Library, and refine it by simply asking to change certain details. It can even resize landscape videos into vertical YouTube Shorts, just like this. They have many other connectors like Blender, SketchUp, and more. Happy creating. Now, on to the next one. The United States wants to turn their military into an AI-first fighting force. So, they've signed deals with almost all the US AI companies. Google has reportedly signed a $200 million classified AI deal with them, allowing the Pentagon to use its AI models for anything they like. Over 600 employees signed an open letter to CEO Sundar Pichai urging the company to reject classified military AI work because it would be used to harm people. In fact, back in 2018, Google publicly promised that its AI would never be used for weapons or surveillance. But in 2025, Google quietly went back on

transcript: Sundar just killed the copypaste workflow forever with one single feature drop. Welcome to day 26 of AI update of the day. You know this pain. Every time AI gives you an output, you spend forever copy pasting into docs and fixing the formatting. Gemini just fixed that problem. They just launched a feature in Gemini app which allows you to ask it to create docs, sheets, slides, PDFs and more directly in your chat. No more copy, pasting or reformatting. Just open the Gemini app, drop in your files, type what you want, and tap download, and the output is actually good. Now, here's the crazy part. You can upload up to 10 files, PDFs, images, handwritten notes, and ask Gemini to transform all of them into something completely new. I asked it to create a study guide from my lecture notes as a PDF. Done. Build a budget tracker with formulas and charts, export as Excel, and it did it. Next, I asked it to summarize these three PDFs into a policy brief and a slide deck. Here's the result. While other models let you export a dock here and there, Gemini is going straight for your entire workflow inside the app. The copy paste era is over. If this one changed how you think about your workflow, I share updates like these in my WhatsApp community link in bio.

transcript: सैम ऑलमैन इस सेइंग दैट एक इंसान ना मिलियन डॉलर कंपनी बना लेगा। हाउ अगर सिंगल पर्सन कंपनी बनाना है तो फर्स्ट जो प्रोडक्ट मैं बना रहा हूं उसके लिए डिमांड है कि नहीं? टू क्या वो यूजर एक्विजिशन जो है वो प्रोडक्ट का डिजिटली होता है कि फिजिकली होता है डिजिटल प्रोडक्ट्स के लिए ये अच्छे से काम करेगा। थ्री प्रोडक्ट का प्राइस ऐसे होना चाहिए जिसके लिए लोग ज्यादा नहीं सोचेंगे खरीदने के लिए। एंड रियलिस्टिकली कितने पैसे बना सकते हैं लोग? अनलिमिटेड। [संगीत] आई थिंक डेटिंग एंड दिस कोच विल बी क्रेजी। शुड वी बिल्ड दैट? आई मीन लाइक राइट नाउ ऑन द कॉल। हां, ठीक है। [संगीत] हाई। सिल्क और आपका नाम? मेरा नाम वैभव है। वैभव अच्छा नाम है। वैसे क्या कर रहे हो आप शाम को? हर महीने करोड़ों रुपए कैसे कमा? यूज़ समथिंग लाइक जूलियस एi। यू कैन कनेक्ट योर शॉपिफाई। जो भी सर्विस यूज करते हो। यू कैन जस्ट कम एंड से हाउ इस माय परफॉरमेंस डूइंग? एंड इट जस्ट टेल यू लाइक अ डाटा एनालिस्ट। फॉर कस्टमर सक्सेस, यू यूजिंग वापी। फॉर रनिंग एड्स यू यूजिंग मैजिक्स। फॉर क्रिएटिंग एड्स यू यूजिंग हिग्स फील्ड। फॉर राइटिंग कोड, यू आर यूजिंग समथिंग लाइक अ रेप्लेट, इमरजेंट। देयर इज़ अ स्टडी अराउंड 80% टू 85% ऑफ़ द पीपल यूज़ एI लेकिन 2 टू 3% आर एबल टू यूज़ एI इफेक्टिवली, द वेट शुड। हैव यू हर्ड ऑफ़ क्लॉडमिक्स? नहीं। मार्क ज़करबर्ग मेटा के अंदर एक कॉम्पटीशन चला रहा है विथ द लीडर बोर्ड। टू शो हु आर द हाईएस्ट यूज़र्स ऑफ़ एआई। पूरा एंप्लाइजस [संगीत] एवरीबडी हैज़ अनलिमिटेड क्रेडिट्स ऑफ़ क्लॉट। एक बंदे ने 2.8 बिलियन टोकंस यूज़ किया है। इन वन मंथ वैल्यू ऑफ़ द टोकन इज़ $1.4 मिलियन। तू सोच रहा होगा भाई कितना क्वेश्चन पूछ रहा है वो? क्वेश्चन [संगीत] नहीं पूछ रहा है वो। ही इज़ रनिंग मल्टीपल एआई एजेंट्स पैरेलली वर्किंग फॉर हिम। तो व्हेन पीपल से जॉब्स ये सब चला जाएगा। एक्चुअली जॉब्स जा नहीं रहा है। जॉब्स इवॉल्व हो रहा है। द प्रॉब्लम इज आपका जॉब इसीलिए जा रहा है क्योंकि आप खुद इवॉल्व नहीं हो रहे हो। व्हाई? मेरा दिमाग घूम रहा है अभी। एंड आई जस्ट लव दिस।

hook_text_english: A guy on Reddit ran the same test on 3 AI models. Three times. Three different winners.
full_text_english: A guy on Reddit ran the same test on 3 AI models.Three times. Three different winners.Nobody is talking about this.He took GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.6, and Opus 4.7. Same workplace questions. No tricks.Round 1. He just asked the questions.Opus 4.6: 84% Opus 4.7: 76% GPT-5.5: 74%GPT-5.5 came last.Round 2. He added ONE line to the prompt. "Sort the evidence real records, casual chatter, pending stuff."12 words. That's it.GPT-5.5: 86% Opus 4.7: 85% Opus 4.6: 85%GPT-5.5 went from last to first.Round 3. He got greedy. Added more rules. More tools. More structure.Every model got worse.Here is the thing.The leaderboard isn't real.The same model can be first or last. Depends entirely on how you ask.→ GPT-5.5 wants a rulebook. Give it structure, it dominates. → Opus 4.6 loves chaos. Hand it a messy problem, it figures it out. → Opus 4.7 refuses to bluff. Perfect for fact-checks.Most people keep switching models. Hoping the next one is smarter.It isn't.Your prompt is the leaderboard.Stop hunting for the best AI. Start writing better instructions.I share updates like these in my free AI community on WhatsApp.Join here ↓ https://lnkd.in/gG2tUqkQ
body_summary_english: A Reddit user tested GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.6, and 4.7 across three rounds with different prompt structures, revealing that minimal prompt engineering (12 additional words) changed performance rankings dramatically—GPT-5.5 jumped from last to first—while over-engineering degraded all models equally.
comments: This is a masterclass in why AI isn't just a technical implementation, but a leadership competency. It proves that the "intelligence" of a model is often a reflection of the clarity of our own instructions. In the corporate world, the goal isn't just to find the smartest tool—it’s to develop the internal capability to guide that tool toward precision.
This is the real insight—model choice matters less than how you use it.Small prompt changes can completely flip outcomes.the edge isn’t the tool, it’s the operator
Big takeaway: it’s not model vs model—it’s prompt vs prompt. Better instructions > better AI.

I strongly agree, and that has also been my experience so far.This feels a lot like process capability in manufacturing.The machine matters.But the method, setup, and operating conditions often matter just as much.An average machine in a controlled process can outperform a better machine in a noisy process.Same with AI.Prompting is essentially process standardisation.Too little structure creates variation.Too much structure creates overprocessing and hurts performance.The goal is not finding the “best” model.It’s reducing variation and designing a repeatable system that gets reliable outputs.

Beautifully said. The real lesson may be that intelligence is not only in the model, but in the meeting between question and mind.A prompt is not just an instruction. It is a frame, a lantern, a way of arranging the room so the answer can find its shape.Perhaps the future belongs less to those who ask the most, and more to those who ask with clarity, care, and intention.

This perfectly shows that model performance isn’t fixed, it’s driven by how clearly we frame the task, and even a simple constraint can flip the results.The real edge isn’t switching models, it’s mastering precise prompting because your instructions are the leaderboard.

Vaibhav Sisinty This is a masterclass in LLM Psychology. 🎯 People treat AI like a magic box, but it’s actually a mirror of the prompt's structure. The fact that GPT-5.5 jumped to the top with just 12 words of 'Categorization' proves that some models are architecture-heavy—they need the 'Lego blocks' to be sorted before they build. The 'Over-optimization' trap in Round 3 is a great warning: Too many rules create Constraint Friction. Stop blaming the tool, start mastering the craft!
This is the part people underestimate—model performance is less about capability and more about framing. Prompt design has effectively become the new interface for intelligence.

This is actually spot on.Most people keep switching models, but rarely fix how they’re asking the question. Same tool, different prompt → completely different outcome.Seen this a lot while working on real use cases  clarity and structure usually matter more than the model itself.

The level of difference is negligible for a common user. While it is important to identify which AI tool does what better, the gap is reducing with time. We are all aware - better prompting gives better results. Instead of engaging in this chaos, we can learn to build the right prompts. The rate at which AI is growing, all LLMs will take care of filling the gaps themseleves, while we understand learning to prompt. Identifying a specialist LLM is a step we can focus on once we master prompts.

So true. I used to write short, random prompts too, but after learning to give proper context and more structured instructions, the results improved a lot.I genuinely believe that good prompting is key to getting the best out of AI. It’s less about the model and more about how clearly you guide it.

This is such a clear reminder that results depend more on how you ask than which tool you use. People keep chasing better models when the real upgrade is in their own thinking and prompts.That small change flipping the outcome says everything.

hook_text_english: Mark Zuckerberg tried to buy an AI tool for ₹17,000 crores. Yesterday, China told him no.
full_text_english: Mark Zuckerberg tried to buy an AI tool for ₹17,000 crores.Yesterday, China told him no. 🤯The tool is called Manus. Meta signed the $2 billion deal back in December.This thing is wild.It books your Amazon orders. Builds full mobile apps from one prompt.Runs 200 research tasks at the same time.Zuck saw it and wanted it inside Meta.But Manus had Chinese roots. And yesterday, the Chinese government killed the deal.₹17,000 crores. Rejected.Here is the thing.The same tool Zuck couldn't buy is sitting in your browser right now.Btw, I tested Manus on 6 real tasks last week Amazon shopping, file cleanup, building a full mobile app, the works. Honestly broke me.Full breakdown video link is below 👇https://lnkd.in/dYTaNwPX

transcript: China just launched an AI product so practical [music] that every American tech company should be panicking. China was facing a very specific problem, >> [music] >> English. For millions of families, especially older generations, it's the one barrier standing between them and better jobs, global education, [music] and opportunity. Parents want their kids to grow up fluent, to work at multinational companies, to access [music] the same internet the rest of the world does. But learning a new language from scratch is years of effort, classes, [music] tutors, time most people simply don't have. So instead of solving this with another app, one company [music] built something you wear, a mask. You put it on and speak in Mandarin. The mask converts it so the audience [music] can only hear fluent English. Parents in China who want their kids to grow up in an English-speaking environment at home have been using it. Think about what this actually [music] unlocks. A working parent who has no time for classes can raise a bilingual child just by wearing a device during [music] the day. Someone from a small city can step into a global one without ever feeling lost in translation. This is what AI looks like when [music] it stops being a product you open and becomes a product you wear. The language barrier has been the oldest wall between human beings. It's time this wall was torn down, too. [music] For more exciting stories like these, you can join my free WhatsApp community. Link in bio.

hook_text_english: Codex is WILDDD!! Ran for 8 hours straight. No heartbeat.
full_text_english: Codex is WILDDD!!Ran for 8 hours straight. No heartbeat.Gave it a brutal task:rewrite backend + data pipelines end to end(security, speed, edge functions, DB schemas)Came back… and it had actually made serious progress.This wasn’t a script.This felt like a 5x sr engineer grinding for 8 hours.Cost: 5,000+ credits 😅I’ve seen 16-hour runs before, but this hit different.Feels like we’re moving from “AI tools” → “AI systems you manage”What’s the longest you’ve let AI run on its own?
body_summary_english: Personal account of running Codex AI system for 8 hours on complex backend rewrite tasks, comparing performance to senior engineer output. Asks audience engagement question about AI runtime.
comments: This is exactly where things start to feel different when AI stops being a tool you prompt and becomes a system you delegate to. The shift isn’t just about speed, it’s about trust, scope, and how much end-to-end responsibility you’re willing to hand over while still steering the outcome.

Eight hours, no heartbeat, and it came back with a canonical data layer and a 10x payload reduction. That's not a demo. That's a backend sprint. What strikes me most is the reliability discipline in the output blocking manual sync without an admin token, clean inbox normalization backlog, failures surfacing clearly instead of silently passing wrong data. Those aren't the things you get from a model that's just autocompleting code. That's something that understood what the system was supposed to do and made opinionated calls about how it should behave. I think the "AI tools → AI systems you manage" framing is exactly right. The mental model flip is real - you stop thinking about what to prompt and start thinking about what to delegate, how to bound it, and how to review what comes back. That's a genuinely different skill set.

Vaibhav Sisinty This is incredible. We’re officially moving from 'Copilots' to 'Digital Employees.'In the growth space, the biggest bottleneck has always been the 'Technical Debt' that slows down scaling. Seeing a system like Codex handle a 'Brutal Task' for 8 hours straight is a glimpse into a future where we don't manage tasks, we manage Outcomes.5,000 credits might seem steep to some, but compared to the 'Opportunity Cost' of a 5x Senior Engineer's time and the weeks of manual refactoring, it’s an absolute bargain. The ROI here isn't just in the code; it’s in the Velocity.The longest I’ve seen a system run 'unsupervised' was for a deep-market sentiment analysis, but seeing it happen in backend architecture is a game-changer. Is this the end of the 'Sprint' as we know it?

Strong signal. We’re clearly moving from task-based AI usage to autonomous execution systems. The real inflection point isn’t model capability alone it’s sustained, multi-hour agentic workflows handling complex engineering tasks. That changes how teams think about ownership, review, and system design. Vaibhav Sisinty

8 hours of work sounds impressive, but what’s the plan for integrating those changes? It’s easy to overlook how critical testing and deployment processes can slow down progress, especially with backend modifications.

This is really impressive Vaibhav! The shift from raw content_items to a canonical data layer in Supabase is a smart architectural decision — especially at the scale where read payload dropped from 7.8MB to 0.81MB. That 10x reduction alone is worth the sprint. The passive classifier running cost-capped is a great touch too. Codex running autonomously for 8 hours on meaningful infra work is a glimpse into how AI-assisted engineering will look in the near future.

I was on your webinar yesterday, so this hits even more.Feels like we’re slowly moving from “using AI” to actually managing it like a team member.At that point, it’s less about prompts… and more about how you think, plan, and review what it does.Curious to see where this goes next.
Rewriting backends is always a massive undertaking. curious about how you approached testing the new pipelines. did you use A/B testing or just a full rollout?

This looks interesting — definitely moving Codex up my list to try.8 hours autonomous on backend + pipelines? That's the kind of delegation that changes workflows.Time to test it on a real build and see if it lives up to the hype.
The shift you mentioned is real. It feels less like coding and more like supervising a process that you can’t fully see in real time.

Looks impressive, though I wonder if it might have skipped a critical test or two, where it just does a simple print to let the test pass through. Happens a lot when you fly blind and ho unsupervised with Ai😅
The leverage is insane, but only if you have clear specs, otherwise it can drift fast

transcript: AI just designed a drug that could give you 13 extra years of waking life. Humans spend 26 years of their lives sleeping. That's one/ird of our life gone. What if we got half that time back without any side effects? You could spend more time doing the things you love. To make this happen, a new AI designed drug is being developed that targets a chemical in your brain called orexin. Orexin is what keeps you awake, activated the right way, and your body needs far less sleep to feel fully rested. Some people on earth are born with a rare genetic mutation. They sleep four to 6 hours a night and they feel completely fine. No fatigue or health problems. Their brains naturally produce the right balance of orexin to function on half the sleep everyone else needs. Over a lifetime that gives them 7 to 13 extra waking years compared to the rest of us. For decades, pharma companies tried to copy this gene. Billions were spent, but every attempt failed. But now with AI in the picture, a small team used it to design a drug that mimics exactly what those rare gene carriers have naturally in 24 hours for about 50,000 rupees. If it works in human trials, every adult on Earth could function on 4 hours of sleep with no downside. Now think about what this unlocks. Sleep stops being a biological requirement and becomes a choice. And the bigger story is this. The era of AI designed medicine just started and the first product might give you back 13 years of your life. For more updates like these, check out my free WhatsApp community.

transcript: The US and China are at war, not with weapons this time, with AI. And Mark Zuckerberg is right at the center of it. For the past four years, Open AI has been leading the AI wave, rolling out breakthrough after breakthrough, from text to images to full-blown AI agents. Meanwhile, Mark Zuckerberg and Meta, they've looked like they're playing catch-up. The metaverse hype faded. VR didn't take off the way people expected. And compared to other tech giants, Meta hasn't really had a clear AI win. So he did what any billionaire does when he's losing. He tried to buy the AI that was quietly beating GPT and clawed for a whopping 17,000 crores. But there was one problem. The tool called Manus AI was built by a Chinese company. And China being China, they weren't having it. For them, this isn't just another chatbot. It's proof that China can win the AI race and they were not about to hand that over to Silicon Valley. So they blocked the deal. They didn't let Zuckerberg buy it. We don't know where this ends up. But what we do know is that this tool is available right now and we wanted to explore it to see what all the hype is about. So you may be wondering what exactly is Manis AI. Think of it as deep research, lovable and claude co-work all rolled into one. It's not just a chatbot. It's an incredible agent and it's widely considered better than anything OpenAI or Anthropic has put out so far. Zuck couldn't buy it, but we can use it. So let's dive in and give it a try. Hi, I'm Vibhav. I run three AI companies and train people across 150 plus countries. I have studied AI thoroughly and now want to teach you how to leverage it and become successful. I also have a free WhatsApp community where I share tools, tips and tricks. So if that interests you, you can click on the link in the description. Quick context for 30 seconds. If you have been using chat GPT or Claude, this is going to make a lot more sense. Every other AI tool you have ever used works the same way. You type something, it types back, and then you go and do the actual work yourself. Chat GBT is like the smart friend you ask for advice. Manus is the employee you actually hire to do the job. It opens browsers, fills forms, writes emails, builds apps, hands you finished work. Once you see it happen, the AI tool you are using today starts to feel kind of slow. Use case one of six. And I want you to look at this very carefully. That is a real cursor on a real Amazon page and there is nobody at the keyboard. So here is what is happening. Manus has a Chrome extension called browser operator. You install it once, you give it permissions and from that point Manus can use your actual browser logged into your accounts. Anything you can do in your browser yourself, Manus can do too. Quick install Chrome web store. Click add. Click authorize. Done. Now, let me give it a real task. I tell it, go to Amazon India. Find me a wireless mouse under 2,000 rupees with at least fourstar ratings. Compare the top three options. add the best one to my cart and stop right before payment so I can review it. That is the entire prompt just like talking to a person. And look at this. Manos opens Amazon, types wireless mouse into the search bar, hits enter, scrolls through results, clicks into a potronix product, reads the reviews, compares prices, adds the one it picks straight to my cart. Nobody touches the keyboard. The first time I saw this happen, I had to pause the screen and rewind it. It feels like science fiction until it is running on your own laptop. Then it hits the Amazon login page and stops, which is fair. That is the one thing it cannot do for me. So I jump in for 10 seconds, type my number, enter the OTP, and Manners picks up exactly where it left off. And then look at this part. It tries to add the mouse to the cart, hits a random Amazon error page, recognizes something went wrong, clicks back, navigates to the cart, and tries again without me saying anything. Old AI gives up at the first er
